summary: Jeff Atwood explores the philosophical difference between Apple and Microsoft's font rendering approaches: Apple prioritizes preserving typeface design even at the cost of blurriness, while Microsoft snaps fonts to the pixel grid for clarity. Citing designer Dave Shea, Atwood argues that Microsoft's ClearType approach better serves current low-DPI displays, as it automates the same per-pixel adjustments designers make manually. He acknowledges that high-resolution displays will eventually make this debate moot, but criticizes Apple for sacrificing present readability for a future that hasn't arrived. The post frames this as a pragmatic choice between competing values rather than a clear right or wrong answer.
key_points: Apple's font rendering preserves typeface design at the cost of blurriness; Microsoft's ClearType prioritizes pixel grid alignment for readability
Professional designers already manually snap type to the pixel grid in tools like Photoshop, which is exactly what ClearType automates
Research supports that snapping fonts to the pixel grid improves reading accuracy, not just aesthetics
High-resolution displays (200+ DPI) will eventually make this debate irrelevant, but the slow pace of display improvements means that day is far off
Typography is genuinely complex enough to justify being called 'computer science'
money_quotes: What do you respect more: the pixel grid, or the font designer?
I don't understand why Apple is asking us to sacrifice the present at the altar of the future.
Can't we have hinting at low resolutions, and accuracy at high resolutions, too?
Typography, if you haven't figured this out by now, is really complicated. It's one of the few areas of 'computer science' that actually justifies the title.

Snapping fonts to a pixel grid may very well be irrelevant when everyone is luxuriating in the glow of their 200 DPI monitors. Until that glorious day arrives, respecting the pixel grid certainly makes text a lot more readable for those of us stuck in the here and now.

Ten years ago I was using a single 17" 1024x768 display; now I'm using three 20" 1600x1200 displays. So you'll forgive me if I'm not overly optimistic about this theoretical jump from 100 DPI to 200 DPI.
key_insight: When designing for current constraints, pragmatic solutions that work well today should take precedence over idealistic approaches optimized for a future that may be slow to arrive.
